steps.title: Define the Strategy Interface
steps.description: Create the contract -- one method that every algorithm must implement. Keep it focused: one interface, one job.

steps.title: Create Concrete Strategies
steps.description: Each algorithm gets its own class. Each class implements the interface. Each class owns ONLY its logic and data.

steps.title: Build the Context
steps.description: The Context holds a reference to the Strategy interface (not concrete class). It delegates work to the strategy. It NEVER knows which concrete strategy it holds.

steps.title: Client Wires It Up
steps.description: The client creates the concrete strategy and injects it into the context. The client is the ONLY one who knows which strategy to use.

java.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (Java)
java.explanation: Java uses interfaces as the Strategy contract. Each concrete strategy implements the interface. The Context holds a reference to the interface type, enabling runtime swapping. The client injects the desired strategy.

cpp.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (C++)
cpp.explanation: C++ uses abstract classes with pure virtual functions. Smart pointers (unique_ptr) handle memory via RAII. The 'override' keyword ensures compile-time safety.

kotlin.title: Strategy Pattern - Payment System (Kotlin)
kotlin.explanation: Kotlin combines concise syntax with strong safety. Constructor properties eliminate boilerplate, override is mandatory, lateinit handles deferred initialization, and lambdas can serve as lightweight strategies for simple cases.
